coredata.dc:title: The synthesis of rhodium/carbon dots nanoparticles and its hydrogenation application
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jie
Chen, Yao
Tan, Jing
Sang, Haitao
Zhang, Liqun
Yue, Dongmei
coredata.dc:description: Rhodium (Rh) nanoparticles have been widely used as potent hydrogenation catalysts. Herein, a new convenient method has been developed to synthesize rhodium nanoparticles, in which carbon dots (CDs) were used both as stabilizing and reducing agents. The fluorescent CDs were prepared by microwave-assisted heating method using chitosan as raw material and the presences of hydroxyl and carbonyl on the surface of CDs were supported by FTIR spectra. Subsequently, CDs could directly reduce Rh3+ to Rh0 without additional reducing and stabilizing agents by heating Rh3+ with CDs for 1h at 120°C. The resulting Rh nanoparticles have an average size of about 2.8nm and the Rh/CDs nanoparticles also retain the fluorescent property of CDs. The hydrogenation activities of Rh/CDs nanoparticles were investigated. The results demonstrated that the nanoparticles had highly catalytic activity in the hydrogenation reaction of hydroxyl-terminated polybutadiene (HTPB) and hydroxy-terminated butadiene-acrylonitrile (HTBN). Also, the presence of CDs could improve the fluorescent properties of rubbers after hydrogenation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rhodium
Chitosan
Carbon dots
Hydrogenation
Fluorescent property

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Catalytic hydrogenation of HTPB by Rh/carbon dots nanoparticles
Catalytic hydrogenation of HTBN by Rh/carbon dots nanoparticles
Catalytic hydrogenation of HTPB by Rh/CDs nanoparticles
Catalytic hydrogenation of HTBN by Rh/CDs nanoparticles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image and size distribution of CDs.
Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs recorded from 280 to 440nm.
XRD spectra of Rh-CD-1, Rh-CD-2 and Rh-CD-3.

(a) TEM image of Rh-CD-1; (b) TEM and HR-TEM images of Rh-CD-2 (particle size distribution inset); (c) TEM and HR-TEM images of Rh-CD-3 (particle size distribution inset); (d) HR-TEM image of Rh-CD-2 (the size distribution of Rh nanoparticles inset); (e) HR-TEM images of Rh-CD-2; (f) HR-TEM images of Rh-CD-3.
Excitation and emission spectra of carbon nanoparticles, Rh-CD-1, Rh-CD-2 and Rh-CD-3.
1HNMR spectra of the hydrogenated products (HTPB) with different catalysts (a: Rh-CD-1; b: Rh-CD-2; c: Rh-CD-3).
1HNMR spectra of the hydrogenated products (HTPB) with different catalysts (a: Rh-CD-a; b: Rh-CD-b; c: Rh-CD-2).
1HNMR spectra of the hydrogenated products (HTBN) with Rh-CD-2 as catalyst.

Fluorescence emission spectra and digital photographs of the hydrogenated products (HTPB) under UV light (a) The original HTPB; (b) HTPB with the HD of 52.9% catalyzed by Rh-CD-2; (c) HTPB with the HD of 91.2% catalyzed by Rh-CD-2; (d) HTPB with the HD of 42.3% catalyzed by Wilkinson’s catalyst; (e) HTPB with the HD of 99.1% catalyzed by Wilkinson’s catalyst.

Fluorescence emission spectra and digital photographs of the hydrogenated products (HTBN) under UV light (a) The original HTBN; (b) HTBN with the HD of 41.2% catalyzed by Rh-CD-2; (c) HTBN with the HD of 84.1% catalyzed by Rh-CD-2; (d) HTBN with the HD of 45.3% catalyzed by Wilkinson’s catalyst; (e) HTBN with the HD of 98.2% catalyzed by Wilkinson’s catalyst.
The preparation conditions of Rh-CD-1, Rh-CD-2, Rh-CD-3.
EDS chemical compositions of Rh-CD-1, Rh-CD-2, Rh-CD-3.
The preparation conditions of Rh-CD-a, Rh-CD-b, Rh-CD-2.

head.ce:title: The synthesis of rhodium/carbon dots nanoparticles and its hydrogenation application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Beijing City on Preparation and Processing of Novel Polymer Materials, Beijing 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Beijing City on Preparation and Processing of Novel Polymer Materials

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rhodium (Rh) nanoparticles have been widely used as potent hydrogenation catalysts. Herein, a new convenient method has been developed to synthesize rhodium nanoparticles, in which carbon dots (CDs) were used both as stabilizing and reducing agents. The fluorescent CDs were prepared by microwave-assisted heating method using chitosan as raw material and the presences of hydroxyl and carbonyl on the surface of CDs were supported by FTIR spectra. Subsequently, CDs could directly reduce Rh3+ to Rh0 without additional reducing and stabilizing agents by heating Rh3+ with CDs for 1h at 120°C. The resulting Rh nanoparticles have an average size of about 2.8nm and the Rh/CDs nanoparticles also retain the fluorescent property of CDs. The hydrogenation activities of Rh/CDs nanoparticles were investigated. The results demonstrated that the nanoparticles had highly catalytic activity in the hydrogenation reaction of hydroxyl-terminated polybutadiene (HTPB) and hydroxy-terminated butadiene-acrylonitrile (HTBN). Also, the presence of CDs could improve the fluorescent properties of rubbers after hydrogenation.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogenation is one of the most commonly used methods for rubber modification that can improve the mechanical, chemical, physical, and thermal properties of raw rubbers [1]. Nowadays, there are many kinds of hydrogenation catalysts. Among them, noble metals such as Rh [2,3] palladium (Pd) [4,5] and ruthenium (Ru) [6,7] nanoparticles are widely used because of their highly catalytic activities [8], mild reaction conditions [9] and recyclability [10]. In the preparation of metal nanoparticles, both reducing and stabilizing agent are used. The common reducing agents include hydrazine hydrate [11,12], NaBH4 [13,14], ascorbic acid [15,16] and citric acid [17], et al. Some of them are toxic and have intense reducibility which can cause the formation of large particles. Those phenomena limit the process of hydrogenation. It is crucial to find environment-friendly reducing agents with appropriate reducibility and pollution-free stabilizing agents in the preparation of metal nanoparticles.

Carbon is one of the most common elements in nature and many kinds of carbon materials such as graphene [18–20] and carbon nanotubes [21,22] have been widely used. Recently, as one of the new carbon materials, CDs have attracted great interests because of their unique functions [23] such as chemical sensing [24,25], drug delivery [26,27], photocatalyst [28,29], low toxicity [30] and bioimaging [31]. Similar to the traditional semiconductor quantum dots, there are many electrons and holes on the surface of CDs. This nature assigns CDs both the role of electron acceptors and electron donors. Thus CDs can serve either as oxidizing agent or reducing agent in some cases [32,33]. Additionally, with numerous hydroxyl, amido or carboxyl on their surface, CDs can coordinate with metal ions and stabilize metal nanoparticles [34]. In recent years, there are some reports about the synthesis of Ag [35] and Au [36] nanoparticles using CDs as reducing agent or stabilizing agent. Chen’s group prepared a new kinds of CDs from natural gas soot and used it as stabilizing agent in the preparation of Ag nanoparticles. Liu’s group found a new one-step synthetic method of silver nanoparticles using CDs both as reducing and stabilizing agents and investigated their antibacterial mechanisms. However, far less attention has been paid to the preparation of metal nanoparticles with high hydrogenation activity, and there are no reports about the synthesis of Rh nanoparticles using CDs as reducing agent or stabilizing agent.

Herein, a new convenient method has been developed to synthesize Rh nanoparticles, in which CDs were used as both stabilizing and reducing agents. One kind of CDs, which have numerous hydroxyl and carboxyl on their surface, were synthesised using chitosan as raw material. The electron-rich groups that can coordinate with Rh3+ and stabilize Rh nanoparticles assigned CDs the role of both reducing agent and stabilizing agent in this study. The resulting Rh nanoparticles had an average size of about 2.8nm and the corresponding Rh/CDs nanoparticles also retained the fluorescent property of CDs. The Rh/CDs nanoparticles had high activity in the hydrogenation reaction of HTPB and HTBN, which could obtain high hydrogenation degree (HD) at 80°C. Also, the presence of CDs can improve the fluorescent property of the rubbers after hydrogenation. Thus, the Rh/CDs nanoparticles synthesized in this study have important application values in the field of functional rubber preparation.

Chitosan (Sigma Aldrich Co. Ltd), acetic acid (analytical grade, Beijing Chemical Company), hydrazine hydrate (reagent grade, Sigma Aldrich Co. Ltd), ascorbic acid (reagent grade, Sigma Aldrich Co. Ltd), RhCl3·3H2O (99.9%, Shanghai Jiuling Chemical Co. Ltd., China), HTPB (Zibo Qilong Chemical Industry), HTBN (Zibo Qilong Chemical Industry), dimethylbenzene (analytical grade, Beijing Chemical Company) were used as-received and without further purification. Wilkinson's catalyst [RhCl(PPh3)3] was prepared according to the reference [37]. Dialysis membrane (1000Da) was purchased from Beijing Biodee Biotechnology Co., Ltd (China) and boiled 20min before using.

CDs were synthesized according to the method reported by Liu’s group [38]. Chitosan (2g) was added to 50g of 2% acetic acid solution and stirred until the chitosan completely dissolved. Then, the mixture was put into a polytetrafluoroethylene vessel and exposed to 600W microwave irradiation at 200°C for 8min. During the irradiation, the faint yellow liquid became brownish red, indicating the formation of CDs. Then, the obtained raw product was centrifuged at a high speed (10000rpm) for 30min to remove the sediment. The supernatant was then filtered through a 0.22μm filter membrane before dialyzed with water for 24h. The final product was obtained through reduced pressure at 55°C for 48h.

720mg CDs were dissolved in 30ml deionized water and stirred for 15min. Meanwhile, 180mg RhCl3·3H2O was dissolved into 20ml deionized water. Then, the RhCl3 aqueous solution was added to the CDs aqueous solution at a rate of 10.0mlH−1 by a peristaltic pump and stirred for 2h. The resulting mixture was added into a polytetrafluoroethylene vessel and reacted at 120°C for 1h. During the reaction, the brownish red liquid became black, indicating the formation of Rh nanoparticles. After reaction, the mixture was centrifuged and dialyzed with water for 24h. Finally, the Rh/CDs nanoparticles were obtained under reduced pressure at 55°C for 12h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic hydrogenation of HTPB by Rh/carbon dots nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: 120mg Rh/CDs nanoparticles were dispersed in 2ml dimethylbenzene by 30min sonication. Then the catalyst solution was added into 6g 20wt% HTPB dimethylbenzene solution. The mixture was placed into a 30ml autoclave and purged with N2 and H2 three times respectively. Then, the hydrogenation was started by heating the autoclave to the reaction temperature and adjusting the agitation speed to 700rpm. During the reaction, the temperature and H2 pressure were kept constant. The reaction was terminated after 8h and the degree of hydrogenation (HD) was measured by 1H NMR spectra on a Bruker AV 400 spectrometer with CDCl3 as solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic hydrogenation of HTBN by Rh/carbon dots nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: 150mg Rh/CDs nanoparticles were dispersed in 2ml dimethylbenzene by 30min sonication. Then the catalyst solution was added into 6g 20% HTBN dimethylbenzene solution. The following steps were the same as that of the hydrogenation of HTPB.

To better analyze the influence of catalyst’s fluorescent property on the hydrogenation products, Wilkinson’s catalyst (nonfluorescent catalyst) has been used in the contrast experiments. During the hydrogenation reaction of HTPB, 12mg Wilkinson’s catalyst was dissolved in 2ml dimethylbenzene by 30min sonication. Then the catalyst solution was added into 6g 20wt% HTPB dimethylbenzene solution. The following steps were the same as the hydrogenation reaction of HTPB by using Rh/CDs nanoparticles as catalyst. During the catalytic hydrogenation of HTBN, the dosage of Wilkinson’s catalyst was 15mg and other conditions were the same as the hydrogenation reaction of HTPB.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on KBr pellets using a Bruker TENSOR 27 FTIR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). The fluorescence test was carried out on an F-7000 fluorescence Spectrophotometer. The UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Jasco J-810 spectropolarimeter. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained with an JEOL JEM-3010 instrument. A JEOL JEM-3010 instrument was employed to obtain the high revolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses were carried out on a Shimadzu XRD-6000 diffractometer. The energy dispersive spectrometry (EDS) was performed with a GENESIS 307 system. 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectrawere were recorded on a Bruker AV400 spectrometer.

The functional groups of the CDs were identified by FTIR spectra (Fig. 2 ). The broad peak at 3436cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibrations of OH. The existence of a sharp peak at 1639cm−1 represents the stretching vibrations of CO. In addition, the absorption peaks at 2853cm−1 and 2924cm−1 are attributed to COOH. Thus, the surface of CDs had lots of electron-rich groups that could coordinate with metal ion and stabilize metal nanoparticles.

The fluorescent property was also examined. As shown in Fig. 3 , the CDs display a bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light and have a quantum efficiency of about 6.65%. In addition, the large energy discrepancy between excitation and emission peaks indicates that the CDs had a semiconductor energy band.

The catalytic activity of CDs in the hydrogenation of HTPB and HTBN was also been tested. The results of hydrogenation were calculated by 1H NMR. As shown in Figs. S1 & S2, the hydrogenation degrees are so low that we can think the original CDs nearly had no catalytic activity in the hydrogenation of HTPB and HTBN.

Herein, a new method had been used to synthesize Rh nanoparticles, in which CDs were used both as stabilizing and reducing agent. To exclude the possibility that the reduction of Rh nanoparticles was induced by chitosan, controled experiment had been performed by mixing RhCl3 with chitosan only. By comparing the XRD (Fig. S3) and EDS (Fig. S4) of two, it can be seen that almost no Rh0 were formed using chitosan as reducing agent under the same condition.

The effect of CDs amount on the properties of Rh/CDs nanoparticles was also investigated. As shown in Table 1 , a series of Rh/CDs nanoparticles were prepared by changing the amount of CDs and were named as “Rh-CD-1”, “Rh-CD-2” and “Rh-CD-3” with the increase of CDs content. The XRD patterns (Fig. 4 ) of the Rh/CDs nanoparticles exhibits diffraction peaks in the positions of 41.1°, 47.8°, 69.8°and 84.4°, which representing the (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes of Rh0 nanoparticles. By comparing XRD curves, it can be seen that Rh-CD-2 and Rh-CD-3 exhibits sharper peaks than Rh-CD-1, indicating better crystallization properties of Rh nanoparticles in those two nanoparticles. The element compositions of the series of Rh/CDs nanoparticles were analysed by EDS. As shown in Table 2 , the Rh contents decrease with the increasing of CDs dosage, which shows that the elements ratio in the products was consistent with the feed ratio.

The size and morphology of the Rh/CDs nanoparticles were examined by TEM and HR-TEM images. As shown in Fig. 5 , Rh-CD-2 and Rh-CD-3 formed cluster structures while Rh-CD-1 generated bulky grain. In the cluster structures of Rh/CDs nanoparticles, CDs served as stabilizers in the formation of Rh/CDs nanoparticles and the metal cores were partly surrounded by CDs (as shown in Fig. 5 (e) & (f)). The sizes of the clusters formed by Rh-CD-2 and Rh-CD-3 are about 23.4nm and 27.8nm, respectively. The further HR-TEM image in Fig. 5 (d) shows that one cluster contained about 20 CDs and Rh nanoparticles while the clusters in Rh-CD-2 had more Rh nanoparticles than those in Rh-CD-3. The fluorescent properties were also examined with an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fig. 6 shows the excitation and emission spectra of the CDs and Rh/CDs nanoparticles dissolved in water. The Rh/CDs nanoparticles had weaker fluorescent intensities than that of the primary CDs and the introducing of Rh element caused a red shift in the fluorescence spectra. The quantum efficiency of Rh/CDs nanoparticles were 3.11%, 3.51% and 4.52% respectively, which were consisted with the content of CDs.

Based on the above results, Rh-CD-2 showed optimal performances with higher Rh content, excellent crystallization properties, smaller Rh sizes and adaptive fluorescent properties, and is possilbe to have the best catalytic activities in the following hydrogation reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic hydrogenation of HTPB by Rh/CDs nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: In this part, the as-prepared Rh/CDs nanoparticle catalysts (Rh-CD-1, Rh-CD-2, Rh-CD-3) were applied in the catalytic hydrogenation of HTPB. During the process of hydrogenation, the HTPB macromolecule and H2 molecules were absorbed on the catalytic active centers and then reacted with each other. The adsorption forces that are determined by H2 pressure, agitation speed, catalyst content and temperature are important to the progress of hydrogenation. Generally, the forces are enhanced with the increase of pressure, agitation and catalyst content. Thus, some commonly used parameters were chosen in three aspects. The H2 pressure (4MPa), agitation speed (700 r/min), mass ratio of [Cat.]/[HTPB] (1.5wt%) were kept constant during the hydrogenation reaction. On the other hand, temperature affects each catalyst in a different way and the influence of temperature was examined separately.

Fig. 7 is the 1H NMR spectra of the hydrogenation products. As shown in Fig. 7, Rh-CD-2 exhibited higher hydrogenation activity than other catalysts, which was consisted with the speculation mentioned above. The best catalytic activity in the hydrogenation reaction for Rh-CD-2 is due to its higher Rh content, smaller Rh sizes, excellent crystallization properties and adaptive fluorescence properties. The effect of the reaction temperature on the catalytic activity also can be seen from the 1H NMR spectra. Both catalysts (Rh-CD-1, Rh-CD-2 and Rh-CD-3) exhibited the highest catalytic activities at 80°C. Considering the fact that the HD decreases with the increase of temperature, the occurrence of catalyst deactivation at high temperature was the reason for the weakening of catalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic hydrogenation of HTBN by Rh/CDs nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the hydrogenation activity of Rh/CDs nanoparticles, HTBN was chosen as the reaction substrate. HTBN has been used as oil resistant rubber due to its polar groups around backbones. However the existence of polar groups can increase the difficulties of hydrogenation modification. Herein, the catalytic hydrogenation of HTBN by Rh-CD-2 and the influence of temperature was also examined. It can be seen from Fig. 9 that Rh-CD-2 had high catalytic activity towards the hydrogenation of HTBN and was able to achieve high HD at 80°C.

The fluorescent properties of hydrogenation products were examined in this part. Both HTPB and HTBN are fluorescent rubbers and show good fluorescent properties before hydrogenation (as shown in Fig. 10 &11). The fluorescent effects is attributed to the conjugated structures in backbones, which are advantageous to the electron excitation. Normally, the conjugated structures are gradually destroyed with the increase of HD, thus the fluorescence intensities gradually decrease until it completely disappear. In this section, the introducing of CDs improved the fluorescent properties of hydrogenation products.

The fluorescence spectra of HTPB are shown in Fig. 10. The curve (a) is the emission spectrum of HTPB before hydrogenation and the peak at 362nm can be attributed to the conjugated structures in backbones. Normally, the conjugated structures are gradually destroyed with the increase of HD, thus the fluorescence intensities gradually decrease (as shown in curve (d)) until they completely disappear (as shown in curve (e)). However, the introducing of Rh/CDs catalyst improved the fluorescent properties of hydrogenation products. With the increase of HD, the peaks caused by the conjugated structures gradually decreased and the peaks induced by CDs (around 450nm) improved the fluorescence intensity of HTPB (as shown in curve (b)). When the HD reached 90%, almost all conjugated structures were destroyed and the product showed a fluorescent property similar to CDs (as shown in curve (c)).

More or less similar to the fluorescent property of HTPB, the emission spectra of HTBN also had a gradual process (Fig. 11 ). The emission peak arose by the conjugated structures of HTBN was around 370nm (as shown in curve (a)), while the peak caused by CDs was around 450nm. With the increase of HD, the emission peak was apparently red-shifted (as shown in curve (b)) and the product showed a fluorescent property similar to CDs when the HD reached 80% (as shown in curve (c)).

In summary, the fluorescent CDs with hydroxyl and carbonyl on the surface was synthesized by using chitosan as carbon source under microwave irradiation. Then, the CDs were used both as reducing and stabilizing agent in the preparation of Rh nanoparticles. The resulting Rh/CDs nanoparticles could form cluster structures and retain the fluorescent property of CDs. The hydrogenation results confirmed that Rh/CDs nanoparticles had highly catalytic activity in the hydrogenation reaction of HTPB and HTBN, which could achieve high HD at 80°C. In addition, the introducing of CDs can improve the fluorescent properties of rubbers after hydrogenation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant no. 20774009 is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2016.11.101.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
